the schoolyard, spoiling for a fight? Are you on fire?

Are you taking the onramp too fast?Tanis MacDonald, Feeding Foxes

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Box: Learning objectives
passages.heading: Learning objectives
passages.text:
- Distinguish between questions (semantic) and interrogatives (syntactic) in form-function analysis

- Identify basic interrogatives using subject-auxiliary inversion

- Explain do-support rules for basic interrogatives lacking auxiliary verbs

- Analyze focused interrogatives and their interrogative phrases (who, what time, where, how often, etc.)

- Recognize when interrogative phrases function as subjects vs non-subjects

- Explain inversion rules in focused interrogatives with non-subject focus

- Apply diagnostic tests for identifying subjects and testing inversion patterns

- Understand the semantic distinction between closed and open questions

- Recognize common student errors in interrogative formation and their sources

- Draw phrase structure trees for basic and focused interrogatives showing gaps and co-indexing

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives
passages.heading: Questions and interrogatives
passages.text:
Tests used in this chapter
This chapter introduces several diagnostic tests for analyzing interrogative structures:

- Declarative-interrogative alternation - Convert between declarative and interrogative forms to identify subjects

- Subject identification test - Use auxiliary placement to determine which NP functions as subject

- Inversion rule testing - Test exceptions to subject-auxiliary inversion (e.g., interrogative phrase subjects)

- Plain form test - Use be vs is in do-support constructions to verify verb forms

- Gap identification - Analyze missing constituents and their syntactic relationships through co-indexing

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Introduction
passages.heading: Introduction
passages.text:
Questions feature prominently in education, no less so in language learning classrooms. Most of them are asked by teachers to students, and many are experienced as awkward by students because of a feeling that the answer is obvious. Consider the following interaction reproduced from Nunn (1999).

T: And then we know that Mr Archer came on his own before... without
anybody. He left his family in England and he came on his own to the
Gulf. So he had to look for a house to live in first before he starts
anything else and as you know he has found a house. And this
house... who can tell me something about this house?
What does does this house consist of? Tell me about this house.
Yes, Mohanned.

S: It's a villa with a large garden.

T: Yes. It's a villa with a large garden. That's right.

I don't know about you, but I find this hard to read. The teacher was probably thinking something perfectly reasonable: I've been talking a lot. Maybe I should give the students a chance, at which point, the question just popped out. Good on Mohanned for helping everyone get on with things. But what was actually achieved? If the goal was to offer the students an opportunity to practice speaking, this seems wholly insufficient. In a class of who knows how many students, one said an eight-word (eight-token, see Section ) sentence that took all of, perhaps, three seconds, before the speaking turn reverted to the teacher. Nor was it likely to clarify the meaning of the text; the teacher even says, as you know, so, was anyone even confused about the nature of the house? And, really, did it matter if they were? It could have been a bungalow or an apartment for all the difference it made to the educational value of the interaction.

As John Searle (1969) observed, we do things with language, and questioning is one kind of thing we do. But in this case, it's akin to asking a guest if they would like a seat when they are already comfortably seated. Such a question is not just redundant but also disrupts the flow of social interaction, highlighting a disconnect between the speaker's intention and the situation. Similarly, in the classroom, the teacher's question, though well-intentioned, ends up creating an awkward moment that does little to advance the educational or social dynamics of the setting.

Of course, it's not quite as cringe-worthy as the seat offer would be. There's a script playing out here, and both the students and the teacher know how it's supposed to go. But that only goes some way to reducing the awkwardness of this meaningless question, and it doesn't mean that the exchange achieved anything worth the class time.

A key insight about the language landscape is that, in focusing on language forms, we risk overlooking the crux of communication: its purpose. Different forms have different purposes; in Searle's words, they perform speech acts. Fostering a classroom environment where students partake in genuine and natural speech acts leads to a more positive and comfortable learning experience.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Questions vs interrogatives
passages.heading: Questions vs interrogatives
passages.text:
A question is something that asks for an answer, whether it gets one or not. But questions are not all linguistic. Sure, they're typically speech acts, or things we do with language, but they don't have to be. If you walk into your kitchen and find a gift on your table, this might pose a number of questions, questions about its source and contents, perhaps about the intended recipient or even about the method of delivery. These don't even have to be formulated into language, let alone uttered to be questions.

Of course we often do use language to ask questions. The most common way to do so is through the use of an interrogative clause such as What's that gift doing there? and Is it for me? (Later in the book we'll deal with subordinate clauses (those that appear inside larger clauses), but for now, we'll focus on main-clause interrogatives.) Recalling the discussion of the many-to-many relationships between categories and functions in Section, we find the same kind of relationship with interrogatives and questions. As the gift example illustrates, questions don't have to be in the form of interrogatives; they don't even have to be linguistic. But, even if they are expressed in language, they can take non-interrogative forms. That's for me? is a question (hence the question mark), but not an interrogative clause. You could even just ask for me? which isn't even a clause, let alone an interrogative.

Conversely, an interrogative doesn't have to be a question. It could be a threat (do you want to try that again?), an offer (how would you like some help?), an instruction (why don't you try again?), an evaluation (is that the best you can do?), or any number of other speech acts.

Terminological hygiene

1.5em

Words (almost) never have a single correct meaning. Obviously, cookie does not mean 'in an upwards direction', but it does mean 'a small file stored on a user's computer by a web browser for tracking or data retrieval purposes' as well as 'a sweet baked treat, often containing ingredients such as chocolate, nuts, or raisins'. I've also taken pains to make clear that just because a term such as phrase has a technical meaning along with non-technical meanings, it's not true that the non-technical meaning is generally wrong. It just depends on what one means: In the appropriate technical environment, the technical meaning should take priority.

That said, careless use of terminology often leads to confusion. One common problem in discussions of language is re-using terms dealing with diverse aspects of language. For instance, in the field of semantics, subject means 'the main topic or the primary entity that the statement is about', but for the syntactician it means 'the function of a constituent in a clause that normally precedes the verb and, in active clauses, normally denotes the actor (if there is one) but in passive clauses, normally denotes whatever is acted upon'. This can be a problem.

Technical terms are often drawn from existing words. Instead of making up a totally new one like predicore or maintent to denote the technical meaning that syntacticians and semanticists have in mind when they use subject, both fields draw on the same non-technical vocabulary. The result is that many people coming to linguistics or language teaching for the first time are confused.

Another example of such confusing terminological overlap is predicate, which, in syntax refers to 'the VP that is the Head of the clause', while in semantics, means 'a relation or property that is ascribed to one or more entities'. (It's the conceptual meaning or function that links the semantic subject to some assertion or description about it. In the example the cat sleeps, the semantic predicate is the concept of sleeping that is attributed to the entity CAT.)

I've avoided this kind of overlapping terminology here, and I encourage you to do so in your teaching too, within reason. This is just to avoid confusion, not because certain uses of terms are correct or incorrect. So, whenever I use the term question, for example, I mean it semantically, and whenever I use interrogative, I mean it syntactically. (For more on how a single label can name two overlapping categories, see the box on p..)

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Main-clause interrogatives
passages.heading: Main-clause interrogatives
passages.text: Main-clause interrogatives (As I mentioned, subordinate interrogative clauses, like I wonder who it could be, will be dealt with in a later chapter.) can be broadly divided into those with an interrogative word (e.g., What is it?) and those without (Could it be a platypus?). I'll use the terms focused for those that include an interrogative word and basic for the others that start with an auxiliary verb. (CGEL uses open and closed for these interrogative types, but since those terms describe semantic question types (see Section ), applying them to syntactic forms can be misleading. This chapter therefore uses basic and focused to describe the form of the clauses.)

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives
passages.heading: Basic interrogatives
passages.text:
Basic interrogatives invert the typical subject-verb order: The declarative Today is a lovely day becomes the basic interrogative Is today a lovely day?

Figure: The subject and the auxiliary verb is switch places in the basic interrogative.

Not just any verb can participate in this inversion; only auxiliary verbs. In contemporary English, you can ask Are they going? with the auxiliary verb are in front of the subject they but not *Go they? with the lexical verb go in front. (Go they was possible until the end of the eighteenth century (Lass1999, 1999).)

I introduced the auxiliary verbs in Section as those that have the NICER properties, and this kind of inversion is the I in NICER (see Table ).

Table: The NICER properties

The other part of the inversion is the subject, which is introduced in Section. There, I mentioned that Subjects typically precede the Head VP, but that in a basic interrogative (Could they be right?), the subject comes immediately after an auxiliary verb. Subject-auxiliary inversion is a key aspect of basic interrogatives, and that's actually kinda weird. Most languages don't make questions this way, and many language learners struggle with it. Instead of asking Is this right? they'll ask This is right? And of course, that's perfectly clear in most contexts, though, by default, it has the pragmatic force of requesting a clarification or expressing a doubt, rather than that of questioning.

Identifying the subject

1.5em

Sometimes, it's hard to find the subject. When you're stuck, alternating between a declarative and a basic interrogative can be helpful. Consider ( and ).

Tuesday could be a good day to fly to Paris.

Could Tuesday be a good day to fly to Paris?

Tuesday, if things work out, you could fly to Paris.

Tuesday, if things work out, could you fly to Paris?

In (a), the first verb is the past-tense modal auxiliary could. Modal auxiliaries don't have third-person singular forms, even in the present, and this is the past. As a result, agreement provides no clues about the subject. But in the basic interrogative in (b), could has moved in front of Tuesday, which almost certainly means that Tuesday is the subject.

(a) also lacks any agreement signals about the subject. But in the basic interrogative in (b), could is in front of you, which means that Tuesday can't be the subject; it must be you.

Here are some more examples with the auxiliary in small caps and the subject in italics. Those in () show different forms of auxiliary be, those in () show different modal auxiliary verbs, and those in () show two forms of auxiliary have (as opposed to the lexical verb have).

Is rain ever warm?

Are the evenings sometimes too bright?

Am I functioning normally?

Last night, was the moon, perhaps, broken?

Will mornings ever surprise you?

Could silence be too loud?

In your experience, have dreams lost their way?

Had the silent night revealed its secrets before?

You can see from these examples that, even with inversion, the auxiliary verb still agrees with the person and number of the subject, when possible: is is the right form to agree with singular rain, are for plural the evenings, and am for first-person singular I.

I included (d) to show that the auxiliary verb doesn't necessarily appear as the first element of the clause; other elements - the NP last night, in this case - may come before it. The auxiliary verb appears before the subject, but not always before everything. The same is true in (a): the auxiliary precedes the subject, but it still follows the PP in your experience.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives > Do support
passages.heading: Do support
passages.text:
In all the examples so far, both the interrogative clauses and their corresponding declaratives would have an auxiliary verb. For example, the declarative counterpart of (a) is Mornings will sometimes surprise you, with the modal auxiliary verb will. But lots of declarative clauses - perhaps most - don't have an auxiliary verb available for subject-auxiliary inversion. In such cases, we recruit do, in its appropriate form. This is called do support.

If inverting the subject and the auxiliary to form a question is a bit odd, magically pulling some form of do out of thin air and having it appear in front of the subject seems even stranger. It's no wonder language learners have problems with this. Still, it is the way we do it in Standard English, as you can see in ().

Do coconuts migrate?

Does the Holy Grail actually exist?

Did Sir Robin bravely run away?

Do knights prefer saying Ni to Hello?

Does the Norwegian Blue pine for the fjords?

There's an extra step to watch out for here. The declarative counterpart of (b) would be The Holy Grail actually exists with that third-person singular -s on the present-tense verb. But in the basic interrogative, it's present-tense do that agrees with the third-person singular, so do does and exists exist. Figure shows this change under do support with third-person, singular, present tense.

Figure: Do support with third-person, singular, present-tense.

Figure shows do support with past tense. Again, the tense is marked on do and not on the lexical verb.

Figure: Do support with past tense.

A verb-form puzzle

1.5em

In Does the Holy Grail actually exist, isn't exist also present tense? So, shouldn't it also agree? In fact, exist is the plain form of the verb: there is only one tensed verb in this clause and that's do (es). This is surprisingly difficult to show, and I can't think of a way to do it with a basic interrogative, but I'll return to the issue after discussing focused interrogatives (Section ). In the meantime, try to think it through.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives > Semantics
passages.heading: Semantics
passages.text:
Basic interrogatives are usually used to ask closed questions. (Again, CGEL uses closed interrogative for the syntactic construction I'm calling the basic interrogative.) One kind of closed question is the yes/no question. If I ask you, Is rain ever warm? then I basically expect you to either confirm or deny that rain is at least sometimes warm. You could say, Yes, it is, or Sometimes, or I think so, but Aardvark is not an answer. I don't know may be a valid response, but it too doesn't qualify as a answer. So, such a question has a closed set of answers that, specific wording aside, boil down to 'yes' or 'no'.

Another kind of closed question offers a set of alternatives. For example, Would you like to eat at 5:30, 7:00, or 7:30? closes the set of answers to one of those three times. Pragmatically, you could respond with indifference, express a love for food, comment on other obligations you have, suggest another time, or decline the offer of dinner altogether. But strictly within the semantic framework, none of those responses qualify as direct answers. The only ones that count as answers are those that state or imply a preference for one of the three times.

Figure: Two types of closed questions.

And on the topic of semantics, I would remind you that, syntactically, basic interrogative clauses are not always semantically questions. They can also be exclamations (Isn't this the best cake you've ever tasted?), expressions of surprise (Are you serious?), insults (Did an infant dress you?), advice (Wouldn't it be better to read the instructions?), and so on. So, while the structure may be interrogative, the intended meaning can diverge from straightforward questioning.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives > Intonation
passages.heading: Intonation
passages.text:
In most dialects, yes/no questions typically have a rising intonation, while alternative questions fall on the final alternative after rising on each previous one, as shown in Figure.

Figure: Typical intonation in yes/no and alternative questions.

This can vary quite a bit though, depending on the attitude behind the question (mere curiosity, doubt, anger, etc.), whether it is said in isolation or as one in a series of questions, and various other factors (Bartels, 1999).

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses
passages.heading: Focused interrogative clauses
passages.text:
Focused interrogatives typically start with an interrogative word: who (including whose and with decreasing frequency whom), what, which, when, where, why, or how. (There are also some older or archaic words that students don't need to learn, including whence, whither, and some compound prepositions such as whereby and wherein.) This is an important group of words, a group that has a central syntactic role in forming interrogative phrases and clauses and a central semantic role in asking questions. But interrogative word isn't a lexical category like pronoun or auxiliary verb; interrogative words belong to many different lexical categories, as shown in Table. (Did you notice which category/ies is/are not represented?)

Table: Lexical categories of interrogative words (based on CGEL)

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Interrogative phrases
passages.heading: Interrogative phrases
passages.text:
The interrogative words appear in interrogative phrases, such as those italicized in (). Sometimes they're the Head and the only word in the phrase, as in (), where who is the Head of an NP. Sometimes they're the Head along with one or more Dependents, as in (). And sometimes, they are a Dependent somewhere in a larger phrase, as in () and (). In both, what functions as the Determiner in the NP what time, but in (), the interrogative phrase is the whole at PP, which includes the NP, which, in turn, includes what.

Who's the kid?

Precisely who called you?

What time did they arrive?

At what time did they arrive?

Common interrogative phrases, apart from the individual words already listed include how many, how often, how long, what day, and since when. There are also a number with how that are semantically interrogative but function syntactically unlike the others. These include How come you did that?, How about if we take a break?, What about this one?

Interrogative phrases work very well as questions all on their own, given the right context. If somebody says, they're arriving tomorrow, then What time? is a perfectly reasonable way to ask what time they are arriving.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Interrogative phrase subjects
passages.heading: Interrogative phrase subjects
passages.text:
Cases like () are the easiest. Who is a pronoun that is the Head of an NP, and that NP functions as the subject of the focused interrogative clause. Starting with He's the kid, we just replace he with who, and we're done.

He's the kid. Who's the kid?

And, as long as the interrogative phrase is the subject, then focused interrogative clauses work like this. () shows various such examples.

Notice that in (), the question is asked in the plural.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting
passages.heading: Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting
passages.text:
But it's not always the case that the interrogative phrase is the subject. When it's not, we're back to subject-auxiliary inversion and sometimes do support, as with the basic interrogatives. And again, this can cause difficulties for students, who often produce uninverted clauses. The inversion is shown in ().

Wh is it It is a pen.

Wh is it It is what?

What is it is [gap]?[fronted]

There are a few things to observe here. First, it functions as the subject in all three examples, even when it's the last word in the clause. Second, a pen functions as a complement in the VP in the declarative (), as does what in (). And then there's the fronted interrogative phrase in (). (I mentioned fronting and stranding back in Section.) That phrase is now in what we'll call Pre function (see Section ), the auxiliary verb is before the subject, and there's just an empty space where the complement in the VP used to be. I'm going to claim that there really is a gap there, and its function really is the same as that of a pen in (a): it's still a complement. See () for a few more examples.

As exemplified in (), when using singular you or I, the declarative and the interrogative typically have contrasting pronouns: I ask about you and you answer I; I ask about myself and you answer you. For this reason, unless you're making this specific point, I avoid you/I examples when explaining interrogatives. That said, as () shows, sometimes we speak to ourselves.

() is also another example of PP fronting. Notice that all the other examples in () have interrogative phrases that start with (and are limited to) an interrogative word. Only the interrogative PP about what starts with a non-interrogative word.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting > Box: Practice
passages.heading: Practice
passages.text:
1.5em

Identify an interrogative phrase that would result in the given answer, and state its function in the interrogative clause. See the example.

x

Example: Brett wrote it.

xxxInterrogative phrase: who, function: Subject

xxxExplanation: One likely question is Who wrote this book? (See Section )

- Answer: I'm from Toronto.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I usually go to bed around 11 PM.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: Taylor did.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I bought it last week.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: She went to the library.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: He's feeling much better now.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I gave it to Jean.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: The tickets were 20.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: Don't worry, it's my phone.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: She's an architect.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: They visited Paris last summer.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I read about it in the newspaper.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting > Testing the inversion rule
passages.heading: Testing the inversion rule
passages.text:
At this point, you might be forming a generalization: All English questions require subject-auxiliary inversion. This seems reasonable given basic interrogatives (Is it raining?) and most focused interrogatives (What did you eat?). But recall the counterexample habit from Chapter 1: when a rule seems too tidy, that's when to hunt for exceptions.

Look back at Section. The examples there - Who's the kid?, What smells?, Which of your friends can come? - all lack inversion. Why? Because when the interrogative phrase itself functions as the subject, there's nothing to invert with. The interrogative phrase is already in subject position, doing subject things like controlling agreement (How many people were there? not *How many people was there?).

This pattern extends beyond simple cases. Consider:

What happened next? (not *What did happen next?)

Who told you that? (not *Who did tell you that?)

Which solution works best? (not *Which solution does work best?)

Students who overgeneralize the inversion rule produce errors like *Who did come to the party? (when asking for new information, not emphasis). The revised rule? English questions require subject-auxiliary inversion except when the interrogative phrase is the subject. This exception isn't rare; subject-position questions are common in classroom discourse (Who knows the answer?, What comes next?, Which group wants to present first?).

Now search for more patterns: Can you find other systematic exceptions to inversion? What about echo questions (You said WHAT?!) or declarative questions with rising intonation (You're coming?)? Each exception refines our understanding of how English actually forms questions.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Do support inversion
passages.heading: Do support inversion
passages.text:
Just as with basic interrogatives, subject-auxiliary inversion works fine with focused interrogatives when there is an auxiliary in the corresponding declarative. But when there isn't, then we once again lean on do for support. See () for a few more examples.

And, just like before, the tense moves from the lexical verb to do, and the lexical verb appears in the plain form. For example, in (), past-tense smelled becomes plain-form smell, and past-tense did is used. Similarly, in (), present-tense get becomes plain-form get, only this time, the forms share a shape, so the change is surreptitious.

A verb-form puzzle

1.5em

Earlier (Section ), I brought up the question of whether or not exist is present tense in Does the Holy Grail actually exist? Now, I can use an interrogative to show you that it's clearly the plain form.

[]The cat is very loud.

[*]Does it ever just is quiet?

[]Does it ever just be quiet?

If we try to use the present-tense form of be, the result is the ungrammatical (b). It's the plain-form be in (c) that is correct. The reason this is so difficult to show is that be is the only verb that has a plain form that is distinct from the plain present-tense form.

Finally, there's the special case of asking about an activity, as in (). In such cases, since the speaker, who may or may not know what the activity is/was, assumes that the listener knows, we use lexical (not auxiliary) do as a stand-in for the activity. The structure, though, is the same. () exhibits do support (auxiliary did together with lexical do), () shows subject-auxiliary inversion with modal auxiliaries, and () does the same but with the progressive aspect, as does () with perfect aspect.

What do/did you do?

What might/can/will you do?

What are/were you doing?

What have/had you done?

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Summary
passages.heading: Summary
passages.text:
So in sum, there are three different types of interrogatives. A decision tree is set out in Figure.

Figure: The choice between interrogative structures.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Semantics
passages.heading: Semantics
passages.text: It will be familiar by now that interrogatives can do more than ask questions, but, in general, the focused interrogatives are used to ask open questions. (CGEL uses the term open interrogatives for focused interrogatives, which typically ask open questions.) These are open in that, when you ask, who did you see? for example, the answer is limited only by the requirement that it be a person or persons. Otherwise, the possibilities are open. The main exception to this is which, which expects answers from a closed set, sometimes explicit (e.g., Which would you like, small, medium, or large?) and sometimes inferrable from the context (e.g., Which one looks best?).

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Intonation
passages.heading: Intonation
passages.text: In general, open questions (and therefore most focused interrogative clauses) end with falling intonation.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
passages.heading: Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
path: Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
passages.text:
Even before students start learning the ins and outs of interrogative clauses, they're able to ask questions: simple interrogative phrases, like how long? for How long will it take? or declarative clauses with rising intonation, such as We have a test? for Do we have a test?

The first is perfectly natural and accurate, and should not be met with an injunction to use complete sentences. Usually, if the student has chosen an interrogative-phrase question, a complete sentence would contain awkward redundancy, making the asker appear stiff and foreign. In the second case though, the student's high-terminal declarative is inappropriate, unless the teacher has already said something about a test. That's because the speech act evoked from such a clause is not a straight-forward closed question. Rather, it's a request for confirmation, clarification, or restatement. The distinction is subtle, but consider Person A's second turn in ().

Person A: I saw a poster about that concert you mentioned last week.

Person B: Oh, the one at the park? Yeah, the whole team's going.

Person A: You're going?

Person B: Yeah, do you want to come?

You're going? is a request to clarify whether 'the team' includes Person B. The question Are you going? might suggest A wasn't paying attention because, from Person B's viewpoint, they've just told A they're going. By framing the question as a declarative, A acknowledges this possibility while asking for clarification. In other words, high-terminal declaratives have their place, but it's not a general question-asking form, and students should understand the difference.

There's one more common questioning strategy that students use. The clauses covered in this chapter are main clauses, but students sometimes employ subordinate interrogative clauses (See Section ) as questions. For example, a student may ask, pointing, how to say this word? This is a subordinate interrogative clause, and as such, it's perfectly fine inside another clause - Could you tell me how to say this word? or I wonder how to say this word. - but it's not Standard to use such clauses on their own to ask questions. Instead we'd use a focused interrogative with do support: How do you say this word?

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Questions in the classroom and classroom questions > Box: Classroom questions
passages.heading: Classroom questions
path: Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
passages.text:
1.5em

A good way to start learning how to ask questions is to memorize some, and a good set to memorize is the set of questions that come up in the language classroom regularly. Here are some ideas, but I'm sure you can think of others.

- What does [gap] mean?

- How do you spell [gap]?

- How do you say this/that word? (pointing)

- How do you say [first-language word] in English?

- Can you give me an example?

- Is this OK?

- Can I say [gap]?

- Do you have a [gap]?

- Can I borrow a [gap]?

- Do we have any homework?

- When is it due?

- Did I spell this right?

- Why do we use this tense here?

- What's the past tense of [gap]?

- What's your email address?

- When do we use [gap]?

- Could you repeat that, please?

- What's the difference between [gap] and [gap]?

- Where can I find out more about [gap]?

- What should we do next?

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Basic interrogatives with subject-auxiliary inversion
passages.heading: Basic interrogatives with subject-auxiliary inversion
passages.text:
Trees may help you think about the structure of basic interrogatives. The tree for the basic interrogative clause Could they be right? is shown in Figure.

Figure: Could they be right: Subj-aux inversion.

The first thing to notice about the trees is the x subscripts after Vaux and GAP. Linguists call such a mark a co-index. It's called a co-index because it's shared between two (or more) elements, and it's called a co-index because, like an index in the back of the book, it points you to something you want to find: if you're wondering where could came from, you check the index, and it tells you it came from the position that now says GAP.

The next notable element is the GAP itself, which is a placeholder. The VP, like all phrases, needs a Head, but in this case, the Head is the GAP that's co-indexed to could, rather than being a verb, as we would usually expect for the Head of a VP. Such GAPs are more important for linguistic theory than for language teaching, but as we see more gaps in this chapter and others that follow, I expect you'll come to see them as important and useful tools for your own thinking about phrase structure.

Having looked at these two crucial elements, let's go through the tree on the left as a review. Starting at the top, we see that it has an auxiliary verb could in Prenucleus function, which is the key element of the basic interrogative clause type. Since prenucleus is an ugly, ponderous word used by virtually nobody outside the CGEL framework, I will simply call it Pre from now on (there will be a Post later). Be sure to understand that subject-auxiliary inversion does not mean that the subject somehow becomes the auxiliary and vice versa. As the tree shows, the subject remains the subject, and the auxiliary verb appears in the Pre function.

Next, there is the Head clause they - be right. At first glance, it may seem strange to have a clause as the Head of another clause. But this is an essential aspect of understanding basic interrogatives. In our example, the main clause functions as the Head of the overall interrogative structure. This arrangement is due to the unique positioning of could in Pre function. It has to be pre-something, and this shows it as preceding the central clause.

In the tree, the Head clause includes a subject NP (they) and a Head VP, represented by the GAP (-) where could would normally be. The clause be right acts as a Complement, and its internal structure is simplified as a triangle here. I encourage you to sketch this tree yourself to grasp its structure fully. Compare your version with Figure, and don't be discouraged if you miss some elements initially. Practice will help you capture all the components accurately.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Basic interrogatives with do support
passages.heading: Basic interrogatives with do support
passages.text: A tree with do support looks, structurally, just like one with subject-auxiliary inversion, such as the tree in Figure. The tree for the basic interrogative Do you like pizza? is shown in Figure. Again, the auxiliary verb do is in Pre function, again the rest of the clause follows as the Head, and again, there's a GAP in the VP.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Focused interrogatives with subject focus
passages.heading: Focused interrogatives with subject focus
passages.text:
Next is the focused interrogative. Again, we'll start with the easiest case, the one with the interrogative phrase as the subject. The example in Figure is Who plays the piano? There's no Pre and no gap, just who functioning as the subject. It couldn't be easier, and language learners rarely make mistakes with this clause structure.

Figure: Tree for who plays the piano.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Focused interrogatives with non-subject focus
passages.heading: Focused interrogatives with non-subject focus
passages.text:
When the interrogative phrase (i.e., the focus) is not the subject, then things get more complex and language learners are much more likely to form questions lacking inversion, like What you can play? In What can you play? in Figure, there are two constituents in Pre function, two gaps, and two different co-indices x and y. Each co-index connects the Pre to its relevant gap.

The functions of the gaps are clearer here. Play is a transitive verb, the kind that takes an Object, so there's an Object gap. While we might say informally that what is the Object of play, that would go against the idea that an Object is a Dependent in the VP, so we give what a different function (Pre) but connect it back to the Object through the x co-index.

passages.anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Summary
passages.heading: Summary
passages.text: Every student error in question formation traces to a specific point in the system: inversion applied where it shouldn't be, do-support omitted, subject focus mishandled. When a student says *Where you are going?, you can now locate the gap (inversion missing) and explain why the student's logic was sound even though the result wasn't..
